sentence: Subjective:
HPI: Mr. Jackson is a 45-year-old male with a history of diabetes type two and mild intermittent asthma, presenting with right elbow pain that started 3 weeks ago after increasing tennis activity.
He describes the pain as an 8/10 when using the elbow and 3/10 when resting.
The pain is sharp, stabbing, and aching, and is worse with movement and better with rest.
He has tried taking Tylenol and ibuprofen, but they do not provide relief.
He has also tried ice, but it did not help.
He has been playing tennis regularly, hitting the ball a lot, and has not had any recent trauma to the elbow.
Past medical history: Mr. Jackson has a history of diabetes type two, mild intermittent asthma, and an allergy to penicillin.
He had his gallbladder removed about 10 years ago by Dr. Nelson.
Review of systems: None mentioned.
Current medications: Metformin 500mg twice a day for diabetes, Flovent twice a day for asthma, and Albuterol as needed for asthma.
Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.
Physical exam: The physical exam was normal with a few exceptions.
On the musculoskeletal exam, there was moderate lateral epicondylar tenderness of the right elbow, and pain with passive range of motion of the right elbow.
There was mild pain with resisted extension of the right wrist.
Test Results: The right elbow x-ray showed no acute fracture or other bony abnormality, no malalignment, and no sign of joint effusion.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: Mr. Jackson has acute lateral epicondylitis of the right elbow, also known as tennis elbow, likely due to overuse from increasing tennis activity.
Problem list:
Acute lateral epicondylitis of the right elbow; diabetes type two; mild intermittent asthma.
Plan:
I prescribed ultram 50mg once per day for the elbow pain.
I recommended rest, stopping tennis for 4-6 weeks, and avoiding repetitive stress in the right arm.
I also recommended icing the right elbow for 20 minutes, 3 times a day, and provided a brace for the right forearm.
I will see Mr. Jackson in follow-up in 4 weeks to reevaluate the elbow and consider additional imaging or physical therapy if needed.
Follow-up:
I will see Mr. Jackson in 4 weeks to reevaluate the elbow and adjust the treatment plan as needed.
